name: Columbus, Ohio
tagline: Ohio's largest city and state capital (~910K residents), rebuilding its technology backbone after the July 2024 Rhysida ransomware attack — with cyber resilience, zero trust, and modernization now the defining IT priorities, run through the Department of Technology.
overview: Columbus is Ohio's capital and largest city, with roughly 910,000 residents, and central IT runs through the Department of Technology (DoT), which supports city departments, two data centers, and tens of thousands of endpoints. The account is defined right now by recovery: in July 2024 the city was hit by a Rhysida ransomware attack that forced a full systems shutdown, exposed the personal data of roughly half a million residents and employees (including crime victims and undercover officers), and left some systems offline for months. The aftermath has reframed DoT's agenda around cybersecurity, network segmentation, zero trust, and resilient infrastructure rather than greenfield smart-city work. Leadership is also in transition — long-tenured CIO Sam Orth retired in mid-2026, with Deputy Director Pam O'Grady serving as interim director while Mayor Andrew Ginther's office searches for a permanent successor. For a Dell SLG seller, Columbus is a large municipal account in active rebuild mode, where security-hardened infrastructure, endpoint, backup/recovery, and modernization carry unusual weight.

budget.intro: Columbus is a large municipal spender whose IT priorities have shifted decisively toward security and resilience since the 2024 attack. The clearest public signal is the $23M zero-trust network program City Council approved in 2025, layered on top of the city's annual operating budget and DoT's ongoing operations.

stats.label: Zero-trust program
stats.note: Council-approved (2025) multi-year build; ~2+ years, touches 20K+ devices across two data centers

stats.label: Population served
stats.note: Largest city in Ohio; scale drives endpoint, identity, and infrastructure footprint

stats.label: People notified
stats.note: Residents/employees whose data was exposed in the July 2024 breach

notes: The city is funding 24 months of Experian credit monitoring for affected individuals — a recurring cost tied to the breach response.
Exact DoT line-item appropriations sit inside the City of Columbus operating budget (Finance and Management); confirm current-year figures before forecasting.
Ohio has moved to require local governments to stand up cybersecurity programs, which adds a compliance driver on top of Columbus's own recovery spend.

initiatives.name: Zero-trust network rollout
initiatives.summary: Council-approved ~$23M program to implement a 'never trust, always verify' architecture across 20,000+ devices and the city's two data centers, with network segmentation to prevent another whole-system shutdown. Multi-year build — the anchor modernization motion.

initiatives.name: Post-ransomware resilience & recovery
initiatives.summary: Hardening backup/recovery, identity, and incident response after the Rhysida attack. Resilient, recoverable infrastructure and segmented architecture are the practical lessons the city is now buying against.

initiatives.name: Infrastructure & data-center modernization
initiatives.summary: Ongoing refresh of compute, storage, and data-center capacity supporting city services — an area DoT has invested in over recent years and that recovery work is accelerating.

initiatives.name: Smart city, data & AI services
initiatives.summary: Building on the Smart Columbus legacy — data analytics/integration, a 311 platform, traffic optimization, and emerging AI uses — though these now sit behind security and resilience in priority.

procurement.intro: Columbus buys through its own Purchasing Office and can also leverage State of Ohio and cooperative vehicles. Confirm Dell's standing on the relevant city, state, and co-op contracts before pursuing a deal.

vehicles.name: Columbus Purchasing Office
vehicles.detail: The city's central acquisition office and bids/solicitations channel (Finance and Management); vendors register via the city's Vendor Services portal and respond to city bids/RFPs.

vehicles.name: State of Ohio DAS contracts / State Term Schedules
vehicles.detail: Ohio Department of Administrative Services (DAS) statewide contracts and State Term Schedules (STS), surfaced through OhioBuys — pre-negotiated state pricing local governments can use.

vehicles.name: Ohio Cooperative Purchasing Program
vehicles.detail: Free, perpetual membership letting political subdivisions buy off established State of Ohio contracts without running their own solicitation — a fast path for IT goods and services.

powerStructure.name: Andrew Ginther
powerStructure.title: Mayor, City of Columbus
powerStructure.note: Executive sponsor; his office owns the search for a permanent DoT director and sets the post-attack recovery agenda.

powerStructure.name: Pam O'Grady
powerStructure.title: Interim Director, Department of Technology
powerStructure.note: Deputy Director serving as interim DoT head after Sam Orth's retirement; the current top IT relationship.

powerStructure.name: Sam Orth
powerStructure.title: Former CIO / DoT Director (retired 2026)
powerStructure.note: Led DoT from 2016 through mid-2026; remaining in an advisory capacity through year-end — institutional context on prior strategy and the breach response.

powerStructure.name: DoT cybersecurity / CISO function
powerStructure.title: Security leadership
powerStructure.note: Owns zero trust, segmentation, and incident response; confirm the current CISO/security lead by name before engaging the security buying center.

powerStructure.name: Columbus Purchasing Office
powerStructure.title: Procurement (Finance and Management)
powerStructure.note: Owns city solicitations and vendor onboarding — the contractual path for any DoT purchase.

news.title: CIO Sam Orth retires after nearly a decade; Pam O'Grady named interim DoT director
news.impact: Leadership transition at the top of city IT — relationship map shifts to the interim director and the mayor's successor search.

news.title: Columbus City Council approves ~$23M zero-trust network program
news.impact: A funded, multi-year security modernization commitment touching 20K+ devices — the anchor opportunity for security-hardened infrastructure.

news.title: One year after the attack, city faces scrutiny over breach response and accountability
news.impact: Public and council pressure keeps cyber resilience and transparency front-of-mind — a tailwind for credible, defensible security investments.

news.title: City confirms Rhysida ransomware breach exposed data of ~500,000 people
news.impact: Established the scale of the incident and drove the pivot to zero trust, segmentation, and recovery spending.

dellActions: Lead with security-hardened infrastructure, endpoint, and identity tied directly to the funded zero-trust program — frame around segmentation and 'never trust, always verify.'
Position resilient backup/recovery and data-center architecture against the lessons of the 2024 whole-system shutdown — recoverability is the proven pain point.
Engage the interim DoT director and the mayor's office now, and track the permanent-CIO search so you're aligned with incoming leadership's priorities.
Confirm Dell's standing on Columbus Purchasing Office, Ohio DAS/State Term Schedule, and Ohio Cooperative Purchasing vehicles to shorten the path to award.
Map the DoT security/CISO buying center and tie proposals to Ohio's local-government cybersecurity-program mandate as an additional compliance driver.
